text: A sixty-eight-year-old man was recently admitted with a small bowel obstruction in the context of a previous gastro-jejunostomy for a duodenal stricture.
The surgeon performs a small bowel resection and adhesionolysis, removing the obstructed segment and releasing adhesions to restore bowel continuity.
He then presented one week post-discharge with an acute abdomen and hemodynamic instability.
A CT scan showed a moderate-sized collection in the left upper quadrant in free air.
The surgical team performs an urgent exploratory laparotomy, opening the abdominal cavity to investigate the cause of the acute abdomen.
Dense adhesions are visible throughout the abdominal cavity, leading to the insertion of a sump drain into the collection. The procedure is then aborted due to the frozen abdomen.
The CT scan image shows oral contrast leaking from the gastric fundus, indicating a perforation in the stomach wall.
This can be seen here on sagittal and coronal views with the extravasation indicated by the green arrows.
During retroflexion, the endoscopic view reveals a visible perforation in the gastric fundus.
The surgeon attempts endoscopic closure of the perforation in the gastric fundus, using an endoscope to visualize and address the defect.
A double channel gastroscope is used to advance a polyloop through one channel while a hemostatic clip is placed through the other channel.
The polyloop is positioned between the prongs of the hemostatic clip within the gastric fundus, as visualized through the gastroscope.
The surgeon deploys a hemostatic clip at one side of the perforation defect in the fundus, visible through the endoscopic view.
The surgeon places a second hemostatic clip on the opposite side of the fundus perforation, positioning the polyloop between the prongs of the clip for secure closure.
The surgeon fully tightens and deploys the polyloop using the gastroscope, securing the closure of the defect with the hemostatic clip.
This is analogous to the placement of an interrupted suture.
The surgeon uses a loop cutter to sever the polyloop tail, ensuring the working space remains unobstructed.
The surgeon continues placing and deploying clips along the defect, repeating the process until the defect is fully closed.
The surgeon identifies a second perforation distal to the first perforation, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon closes the second perforation using the same technique previously applied, ensuring the defect is completely sealed.
The surgeon examines both repaired areas closely, confirming that the tissue defects are completely closed with no visible openings.
Follow-up CT scan with oral contrast confirmed repair of the perforations.
Green arrows indicate the area of repair on the tissue, highlighting the site of the surgical intervention.
